questions.question: Explain the decision tree model for analysis of comparison-based algorithms. What are its fundamental assumptions?
keyPoints: Decision tree is a binary tree representing all possible comparison sequences
Internal nodes represent comparisons between elements
Leaves represent distinct outcomes/solutions
Assumption: Algorithm gains information only through pairwise comparisons
No other operations provide information about element order

questions.question: Prove that any comparison-based sorting algorithm requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case.
keyPoints: Decision tree for sorting must have at least n! leaves (one per permutation)
A binary tree of height h can have at most 2^h leaves
Therefore, height h ≥ log₂(n!)
Using Stirling's approximation: log₂(n!) ≈ n log₂ n - 1.44n
Hence Ω(n log n) comparisons required

questions.question: What is the information-theoretic lower bound? How is it applied to prove lower bounds in decision tree model?
keyPoints: Based on information theory - each comparison yields at most 1 bit of information
To distinguish m possible outcomes, need at least ⌈log₂m⌉ comparisons
For sorting: m = n! permutations, so lower bound = ⌈log₂(n!)⌉
For searching: m = n+1 outcomes, so lower bound = ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉
This provides theoretical minimum regardless of algorithm design

questions.question: Derive the lower bound for searching a sorted array using decision trees.
keyPoints: Searching must distinguish between n possible elements + not found = n+1 outcomes
Decision tree must have at least n+1 leaves
A binary tree of height h can have at most 2^h leaves
Therefore, 2^h ≥ n+1, giving h ≥ ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉
Binary search achieves this bound, so it is optimal

questions.question: Explain Stirling's approximation and its role in decision tree analysis.
keyPoints: Stirling's formula: n! ≈ √(2πn)(n/e)^n
Taking log base 2: log₂(n!) ≈ n log₂ n - n log₂ e ≈ n log₂ n - 1.44n
This simplifies the calculation of log₂(n!)
Used to derive the asymptotic lower bound n log n for sorting
Provides the -1.44n constant term in the bound

questions.question: Why can't comparison-based sorting algorithms achieve better than O(n log n) time complexity?
keyPoints: Every comparison-based algorithm corresponds to a decision tree
The decision tree must have n! leaves to distinguish all permutations
Minimum height of such a tree is ⌈log₂(n!)⌉ = Ω(n log n)
This lower bound is independent of algorithm design
Only non-comparison sorts (counting, radix) can bypass this limit

questions.question: What is an optimal decision tree? Give an example of an algorithm that achieves optimal decision tree for a problem.
keyPoints: An optimal decision tree has minimum possible height for the problem
For searching in sorted array, binary search achieves optimal height ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉
For sorting, merge sort achieves optimal Θ(n log n) bound
Optimal trees provide best worst-case performance
Not all problems have algorithms matching the lower bound exactly

questions.question: Define decision tree. How is it used to establish lower bounds for comparison-based algorithms? Derive the lower bound for sorting n elements.
keyPoints: Definition: Binary tree model representing comparison sequences in algorithms
Each internal node = comparison, branches = outcomes, leaves = solutions
To establish lower bound: count possible outcomes, find minimum tree height
For sorting: n! permutations → n! leaves required
Height h ≥ ⌈log₂(n!)⌉ ≥ n log₂ n - 1.44n = Ω(n log n)
Stirling's approximation proof shown
Merge sort achieves this bound - it's optimal
Distinguish worst-case vs average-case analysis
Discuss information-theoretic perspective

questions.question: Compare the decision tree approach with other methods of algorithm analysis. Why are lower bounds important in algorithm design?
keyPoints: Decision tree model focuses on comparison count only
Other methods: counting operations, RAM model, external memory model
Lower bounds tell us optimality limits - no algorithm can do better
Helps identify when to stop optimizing - reached theoretical limit
Guides algorithm design toward optimal solutions
Practical importance: shows when to use non-comparison methods
Example: sorting - when n log n reached, focus on constants
Comparison with: adversarial arguments, counting arguments
Decision trees provide mathematical foundation for limits

questions.question: Explain the decision tree model for searching. Prove that binary search is optimal for searching in a sorted array.
keyPoints: Decision tree for searching has n+1 leaves (n elements + not found)
Each comparison splits the search space into two
Minimum height h satisfies 2^h ≥ n+1
Hence h ≥ ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉
Binary search creates balanced tree with exactly ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉ height
Show binary search tree structure and how it achieves bound
Proof by contradiction: any algorithm must have at least this height
Optimality established through lower bound matching
Average case analysis also approximately log₂n

questions.question: Discuss the average-case lower bound for comparison-based sorting. Is it possible to achieve better average-case performance than worst-case?
keyPoints: Average-case considers expected comparisons over all inputs
Lower bound Ω(n log n) applies to average-case as well
Reason: decision tree must still distinguish n! permutations
Average height of any comparison tree ≥ log₂(n!)
Different from worst-case: some algorithms have better average than worst
Example: quick sort has average O(n log n) but worst O(n²)
But no algorithm can have average better than Ω(n log n)
Merge sort achieves both optimal worst and average case
Information theory applies to both analyses
